title: Ethiopia’s Trade Union Movement Is Growing Stronger
summary: When the General Assembly of the Confederation of Ethiopian Trade Unions (CETU) met in December 2025, it confirmed that the membership of its affiliated unions had, for the first time in Ethiopian history, surpassed one million workers, organized in 2,653 basic enterprise unions. This denotes remarkable growth for a trade union movement whose membership figures […]
reason: A million workers building new organizational circuits outside state and market intermediaries is a concrete example of routing-level change in a Global South context rarely covered.
angle: Use the routing architecture tool to show how trade union confederation-building creates durable flows of solidarity, resources, and collective intelligence that bypass both state patronage and employer capture.

title: Gambling Is Thousands of Years Older Than We Thought, Rewriting Human Evolution
summary: Native Americans were playing dice and other games of chance many millennia before any known cultures elsewhere.
reason: An interesting archaeological finding but it illuminates nothing about capture, enclosure, commons, or resistance routing — it requires the framework to be forced onto it.

title: CA Should Send Prisoners Home Instead of Spending Millions on New Facilities
summary: A new facility celebrated by Gov. Gavin Newsom will disrupt prisoner-led programming and normalize our captivity. The post CA Should Send Prisoners Home Instead of Spending Millions on New Facilities appeared first on The Appeal .
reason: The story shows the alibi structure of prison reform in operation: new facilities are framed as progress while destroying the prisoner-led commons (self-organized programming) they nominally replace, making the enclosure mechanism visible.
angle: Use alibi structure to show how facility construction requires you to believe 'humane infrastructure' is reform, while the routing question reveals it redirects resources away from release and toward permanent captivity infrastructure.

title: ‘Not quite Greggs’: TikTok creators put London’s ‘gentrified’ bakeries to the test
summary: Viral reviews of artisan cafes across the capital are sparking a debate over cost, culture – and who gets a slice of the city The video that started it all was innocuous enough: a woman in her 20s posted on TikTok about how she spends a perfect weekend in north London. On her list were the bakeries Jolene and Gail’s, and the De Beauvoir Deli. The reaction, however, was anything but. Many locals commented that they had never heard of the businesses she mentioned. One north Londoner, Moses Combe, 21, was equally incredulous. “If this is where all the north London girlies come in the morning, I’d be a bit surprised,” he said in a viral video. Continue reading...
reason: The story touches on class-based enclosure of urban space but stays primarily in the representational register — it's a content debate about culture and cost rather than showing routing-level alternatives or the capture mechanism in structural operation.
